data.title: T-Mobile signals $18.4B-$18.8B 2026 adjusted free cash flow as it maintains ~$77B service revenue outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: T-Mobile US (TMUS) Q2 2026

Management view

  • "Q2 was another extraordinary quarter of executing on our strategy" (CEO, President & Director Srinivasan Gopalan) and "nothing demonstrates this better than our NPS, a record high 46 this quarter" (CEO Gopalan), which he called "the highest NPS in wireless ever across the big 3 carriers."
  • "Our share" grew "across every single cohort within the top 100 markets and also in smaller markets and rural areas" (CEO Gopalan), which he sized as "40% approximately of the population" where T-Mobile has "just 24% total share of households," adding that "the integration is going great" for the UScellular acquisition.
  • On broadband, management framed 5G home internet as moving upscale: "5G broadband has rapidly become a premium broadband offering" (CEO Gopalan) and, with the latest router, "we're delivering download speeds roughly equivalent to fiber-to-the-home when both are used over WiFi" (CEO Gopalan).
  • "Our unmatched value proposition drove postpaid net account additions of 277,000 in Q2, alongside 2% ARPA growth year-over-year" (CEO Gopalan), and he added "over 60% of customers on new accounts" chose premium plans.
  • "We continue to expect postpaid account net additions to be between 950,000 and 1,050,000" (Executive VP & CFO Peter Osvaldik) and "as part of our full year plan" the company expects "our Q3 rate plan modernization would result in a temporary elevated account churn profile" with "Q3 net postpaid account additions to be approximately 250,000" (CFO Osvaldik).

Outlook

  • "We continue to expect to deliver full year service revenues of approximately $77 billion" (CFO Osvaldik) and "Q3 expectations of approximately $19.3 billion" (CFO Osvaldik), alongside "strong postpaid ARPA growth of between 2.5% and 3% this year" (CFO Osvaldik).
  • "We continue to expect core adjusted EBITDA to be between $37.1 billion and $37.5 billion" (CFO Osvaldik) with "Q3 core adjusted EBITDA of approximately $9.4 billion" (CFO Osvaldik).
  • "We are increasing our adjusted free cash flow guidance to now be between $18.4 billion and $18.8 billion" (CFO Osvaldik), which he said was "primarily driven by lower cash income taxes."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management reiterated the same full-year ranges for postpaid net account adds, service revenues, ARPA growth, core adjusted EBITDA, and cash CapEx, while raising adjusted free cash flow from "between $18.1 billion and $18.7 billion" (CFO Osvaldik, Q1) to "between $18.4 billion and $18.8 billion" (CFO Osvaldik, Q2).

Financial results

  • "Postpaid service revenue up 13%. Total service revenue up 9%" (CEO Gopalan) and "core adjusted EBITDA up 12%, with industry-leading free cash flow margin of 25%" (CEO Gopalan).
  • "We had postpaid phone churn of 0.85%" (CFO Osvaldik) as management emphasized customer experience, including "we ended the quarter with over 30 million monthly active users" for T-Life (CEO Gopalan).
  • "In addition to our dividend" the company "repurchased an incremental $2.5 billion in Q2 and through July 17" (CFO Osvaldik) and "since beginning our share buyback program in late 2022, we have repurchased 253 million shares" (CFO Osvaldik), reducing shares outstanding "to 1.07 billion" (CFO Osvaldik).

Q&A

  • Sean Diffley, Morgan Stanley: asked about volume vs. price; CEO Gopalan said the balance "really centers around CLV" and "we titrate that balance very carefully," while CFO Osvaldik added "ex M&A, postpaid ARPA actually grew 3.7%" year-over-year.
  • Michael Funk, BofA: asked about device subsidies into holiday; CEO Gopalan said "our intention...is not to increase our subsidy levels" and "customers will have to pay more" as "memory price increases" lift device prices.
  • Craig Moffett, MoffettNathanson: asked if spectrum supports FWA vs. AI traffic; CEO Gopalan said FWA is "a complete fallow capacity model" and spectrum would be incremental because "our guidance didn't build any of that in," while CTO John Saw said AI training is "confined" to data centers and "we have not seen any surge in mobile traffic due to AI."
  • John Hodulik, UBS: asked about EBITDA phasing and buybacks; CFO Osvaldik said the quarterly pattern reflects "timing of when acquisitions occurred" and on buybacks "I'm not here to guide" a fixed quarterly level.
  • Peter Supino, Wolfe: asked if low-earth-orbit satellite pricing affected FWA; Chief Broadband, Enterprise & Emerging Business Officer André Almeida said "our product is significantly better" and "2/3 of our customers are in the top 100 markets" where satellite capacity is constrained.
  • Kannan Venkateshwar, Barclays: asked why not lean into satellite; CEO Gopalan said "FWA is a far superior product" and T-Mobile is "not seeing that incremental differentiation" from broader satellite partnering.
  • Kutgun Maral, Evercore: asked about fiber JVs; Almeida said "we're getting to close to 20% penetration over the first 12 months" and CEO Gopalan said "we're not chasing some vanity number of homes passed."
  • Michael Ng, Goldman Sachs: asked about service revenue ramp and wholesale; CFO Osvaldik said growth drivers include "ARPA expansion" and added wholesale is "roughly flat for the full year" due to "the roll-off of DISH and TracFone"; COO Jon Freier said in prepaid, higher-end customers are migrating to postpaid, which is "a good thing for us."
  • Sebastiano Petti, JPMorgan: asked bundling and D2D JV timing; CEO Gopalan said D2D JV talks are "progressing rapidly" and "we should have an update...shortly," while Almeida said broadband-with-mobile attachment is "closer to 70%."
  • Bryan Kraft, Deutsche Bank: asked prepaid decline drivers and Starlink exclusivity; CEO Gopalan said satellite usage was "0.0002%" previously and "0.0003%" in busy summer months, and management expects the JV to source "the vast majority" of future D2D.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-slightly skeptical, pressing on device subsidy intensity, EBITDA deceleration optics, spectrum spend vs. buybacks, and competitive threats from LEO; examples included concerns that "FWA...is now consuming most of the capacity" (Craig Moffett, MoffettNathanson) and questions on whether buybacks stay near "$2 billion" levels (John Hodulik, UBS).
  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and largely confident in Q&A, repeatedly anchoring on differentiation and capacity; CEO Gopalan said "we feel incredibly good" about the "15 million" FWA target and CFO Osvaldik said "we continue to see strong momentum in the business."
  • Versus the prior quarter, Q2 included more explicit near-term caveats on churn from rate plan modernization, while still framing guidance as stable; CFO Osvaldik reiterated Q3 churn expectations and the company’s approach to "providing updates throughout the year as warranted" (CFO Osvaldik).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The prior quarter featured guidance increases and a higher emphasis on new fiber JV announcements; Q2 emphasized execution against February ambitions, added a Q3 modernization churn callout, and raised only adjusted free cash flow.
  • Management added a notable leadership update in Q2: "Chris Sambar" will join "as our Chief Enterprise Officer" in August (CEO Gopalan); this role and hire were not cited in Q1.
  • Analyst focus shifted from Q1 topics like governance rumors and synergy progress to Q2 topics centered on pricing vs. volume, device subsidy posture, spectrum auctions (C-band 2.0 and 2.7), and competitive risk from LEO.

Risks and concerns

  • "Our Q3 rate plan modernization would result in a temporary elevated account churn profile" (CFO Osvaldik), which management tied to a short-term net add expectation of "approximately 250,000" (CFO Osvaldik).
  • CEO Gopalan tied higher handset prices to industry inputs: "memory price increases are resulting in higher prices for smartphones across the board" and said T-Mobile does "not" intend "to increase our subsidy levels."
  • Management highlighted upcoming spectrum auctions as a capital-allocation consideration: CFO Osvaldik said T-Mobile is "thoughtfully maintaining a capital envelope" for "upcoming spectrum opportunities in both 2027 and 2028," including "C-band 2.0 and 2.7 gigahertz."

Final takeaway

Management presented Q2 as continued share gains and strong financial execution, while keeping the core 2026 guidance framework intact and raising adjusted free cash flow to $18.4 billion-$18.8 billion. In Q&A, executives repeatedly framed pricing and promotion decisions through CLV and premium mix, flagged a planned Q3 churn bump tied to rate plan modernization, and positioned spectrum opportunities in 2027-2028 as additive to long-term network leadership without changing the fallow-capacity approach underpinning the 15 million fixed wireless target.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Howden Joinery Group Plc 1H GAAP EPS of £0.16, revenue of £1.03B
Horizon projects 4.30%-4.35% net interest margin in H2 2026 while keeping mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Horizon Bancorp, Inc. (HBNC) Q2 2026

Management view

  • CEO Thomas Prame said Q2 results showed “a net interest margin in the mid-4.30% range, strong fee income performance and excellent credit trends,” and highlighted capital levels of “CET1 of 11.09% and total risk-based capital of 15.01% at the end of the quarter.”

  • CEO Prame framed first-half balance-sheet growth as “year-to-date deposit growth of approximately 5% annualized, coupled with quality lending growth of approximately 4% annualized,” adding that “commercial loan performance” ran at “5.7% annualized in the second quarter.”

  • CEO Prame disclosed a litigation-related accrual, saying, “the company did establish an accrual for legal expenses related to an unfavorable litigation decision in the second quarter,” and added, “we anticipate this accrual will remain in place until the company finalizes its appeal process in subsequent quarters.”

  • Executive VP Lynn Kerber said loans held for investment ended “just under $5 billion,” up “approximately $81 million or 6.6% annualized,” and emphasized mix: “The vast majority of our growth came from C&I lending, which increased approximately $62 million (sic) [ $63 million ] to 31% of the commercial portfolio.”

  • Kerber described credit trends as stable, including “net charge-offs… approximately $605,000 or just 5 basis points annualized,” “substandard loans totaled $64.6 million or 1.3%,” and “nonperforming loans declined during the quarter to $34.9 million or 0.70% of loans.”

  • CFO John Stewart said, “the net interest margin expanded by another 8 basis points in Q2 to 4.37%,” and attributed part of the move to average interest-earning cash being “down about $59 million.”

Outlook

  • CFO Stewart maintained the core balance-sheet growth stance: “Period-end loan and deposit balances are still expected to grow mid-single digits,” and reiterated, “balance sheet growth will be driven by deposit growth going forward, and this strategy has not changed.”

  • CFO Stewart guided, “we anticipate the margin percentage to be in the range of 4.3% to 4.35% over the second half of the year,” while noting rate-assumption changes: “the outlook on Slide 13 now includes… 25 basis point Fed hike later in the year compared with 2 cuts in our initial 2026 outlook in January and no rate changes in our April update.”

  • CFO Stewart described pricing dynamics supporting the outlook, including “the weighted average new production rate on total loans was about 6.75% in Q2,” and expected cash flows of “approximately $150 million per quarter coming off in the 6.2% range.”

  • CFO Stewart said expense guidance was refined around the legal item: “Excluding the $3.1 million legal charge in the second quarter, expenses are now expected to be in the low to mid-$160 million range for the full year.”

Financial results

  • CFO Stewart reported noninterest income strength: “noninterest income results were strong during the quarter, growing 10% compared with the year ago period, led by fiduciary activities and mortgage, which each grew by about 20% year-on-year.”

  • CFO Stewart detailed cost items and operating leverage focus: “expenses came in at $43.8 million, which includes the previously announced $3.1 million legal charge,” and added, “excluding this item, expenses were modestly better than expectations and largely unchanged from the prior quarter.”

  • CEO Prame quantified the per-share impact of the legal accrual, stating, “Excluding this one-time event, which represents earnings of approximately $0.05 per share in the quarter, the second quarter results were positive on many fronts.”

Q&A

  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Asked whether funding costs creeping up would pressure margin vs. the 4.30% to 4.35% range; CFO Stewart responded, “Yes, not really,” and said the margin-range driver was “purely a mix issue with cash,” adding, “it’s nothing more than that.”

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Asked why hold higher cash; CFO Stewart said, “I don’t think it’s actively managing cash,” calling it “the timing of the ebbs and flows of deposit balances.” Race also pressed on premium new-loan pricing; Kerber said, “Pricing has been… fluid, dynamic,” and emphasized “trying to manage overall pricing for your ‘average credit’.”

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Asked about target capital ratios and deployment (buybacks/M&A); CEO Prame said, “We do not have a published capital level that we’re shooting for,” and cautioned, “capital is not going to burn a hole in our pocket.” CFO Stewart added, “we’ve got 1.5 million shares left on the prior authorization.”

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde: Asked about opportunity from a newly announced local deal; CEO Prame said, “any time that there’s going to be a transaction in the marketplace… we… believe there’s going to be an upside for us.” Nosal also asked what is more competitive—lending or deposits; Prame said “large commercial real estate… is extremely competitive,” while noting elevated CD rates in some markets.

  • Brandon Rud, Stephens: Asked if CRE payoffs were driven by sales or competitive refinancing; Kerber said, “it’s mostly attributable to our customers fulfilling their business purpose,” while adding, “there are a couple of deals that we did let go over pricing structure.” Rud also asked about commercial NPL mix; Kerber said, “No concerns. It’s just working through the process.”

  • Brian Martin, Brean: Asked about CRE concentration targets; CFO Stewart said, “the concentration levels are about 235%,” and added, “we’ve got plenty of room there currently.” Martin also asked about payoff outlook; Kerber said, “Q3 may be similar to Q2,” but “Q4, I would expect they would return to more of our normal pace.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on margin mechanics, competitive pricing, payoff drivers, and capital deployment; Del Monte’s margin question included a check on whether rising funding costs could “weigh on the margin,” and Race repeatedly challenged assumptions around cash, pricing, and capital.

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and explanatory; CFO Stewart used clarifying language in response to margin concern (“Yes, not really. Let me see if I can clarify.”), while CEO Prame emphasized discipline on capital (“capital is not going to burn a hole in our pocket”) and relationship banking (“Our strategy is not to be lead the market with rate”).

  • Versus Q1, the dialogue stayed focused on similar investor pressure points (margin/cash, deposit costs, loan mix, capital optionality), but Q2 added a more explicit, repeated explanation that the margin outlook shift is “purely a mix issue with cash.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Management shifted from highlighting Q1 deposit surge (“over $147 million in growth or 11% annualized,” CEO Prame in Q1) to describing Q2 as a period of deploying liquidity to support lending, with Q2 deposits “up $125 million, representing a 4.8% annualized growth rate” year-to-date (CEO Prame in Q2).

  • Net interest margin increased to 4.37% in Q2 (CFO Stewart) from 4.29% in Q1 (CEO Prame/CFO Stewart), while the second-half margin framework narrowed to 4.30% to 4.35% (Q2) from Q1’s full-year 4.25% to 4.35% range (CFO Stewart in Q1).

  • Q2 introduced a discrete legal expense item (the “$3.1 million legal charge,” CFO Stewart; “approximately $0.05 per share,” CEO Prame) that was not discussed as a P&L factor in Q1.

  • Capital build messaging remained consistent quarter to quarter, with CET1 moving to 11.09% in Q2 (CEO Prame/CFO Stewart) from 10.82% in Q1 (CEO Prame/CFO Stewart), alongside continued emphasis on disciplined deployment and optionality.

Risks and concerns

  • CEO Prame flagged ongoing uncertainty around litigation costs, stating the accrual related to the “unfavorable litigation decision” is expected to remain until the appeal process is finalized.

  • Management cited a “competitive market environment” in lending (Kerber) and highlighted that “large commercial real estate… is extremely competitive” in pricing and structure (CEO Prame), with selective walk-aways from deals “over pricing structure” (Kerber).

  • CFO Stewart reiterated deposit-cost sensitivity to rate conditions, stating it “continues to be our expectation that interest-bearing deposit costs trend modestly higher from here with no additional rate cuts,” even as the company uses “some promotional deposit pricing.”

Final takeaway

Management described Q2 as extending first-half momentum through stronger net interest margin, sustained loan and deposit growth, and stable credit, while absorbing a $3.1 million legal charge tied to an appealed litigation decision. For the second half of 2026, management kept its mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth expectations, projected a 4.30% to 4.35% net interest margin range largely driven by cash mix, maintained full-year fee income in the mid-$40 million range, and outlined continued capital build alongside ongoing evaluation of buybacks and disciplined M&A.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Midday Need to Know: Crude breaches $100, jobless claims decline & more
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages slid on Thursday as tensions in the Middle East lifted oil prices to above $100 per barrel, while investors digested the latest quarterly results from giants such as Tesla (TSLA) and Alphabet (GOOGL), (GOOG).

Now, here are four news stories that broke in the morning to watch out for:

Brent crude breaches $100 on Red Sea shipping attacks: Brent crude  surged 6.6% to $100.36 a barrel and Nymex crude jumped 5.4% to $91.53 after Iran-backed Houthi militants claimed attacks on two Saudi oil tankers attempting to transit the Bab al-Mandeb strait connecting the Red Sea to the Indian Ocean. The strikes open a volatile new front in the conflict, imperiling the critical detour route Saudi Arabia has relied on to keep oil flowing amid disruptions that have largely closed the Strait of Hormuz. If the Bab al-Mandeb route is shut, vessels can still reach Asian customers but only after a massive detour through the Suez Canal and around Africa, adding 10-15 days to journeys and tying up more tanker capacity.

President Trump considers massive military escalation in Iran: President Donald Trump told Axios he is close to deciding whether to authorize another major military operation against Iran, potentially larger than "Operation Epic Fury." Trump said Israel would join the attack if asked but added "we don't need anybody" to launch a new operation against Tehran. The comments come as the U.S. completed its 12th straight day of strikes against Iranian targets on Wednesday, and reports indicate the Pentagon is surging additional forces, medics, and weaponry to the Middle East to provide expanded military options.

Initial jobless claims decline sharply to 187,000: Weekly initial jobless claims  fell by 22,000 to a seasonally adjusted 187,000 for the week ended July 18, comfortably beating the 211,000 consensus estimate and down from the prior week's revised 209,000. The four-week moving average dropped to 207,500, a decrease of 7,250 from the previous period. Continuing claims for the week ended July 11 edged down to 1.796 million, while the insured unemployment rate held steady at 1.2%.

House narrowly approves $1.15T defense authorization bill: The House of Representatives  passed the $1.15 trillion fiscal 2027 National Defense Authorization Act in a tight 216-212 vote on Thursday, with most Republicans supporting and most Democrats opposing the measure. The legislation would fund military procurement, weapons development, and pay raises of up to 7% for service members. The bill faces an uncertain path in the Senate after Republicans attached separate voting legislation backed by President Trump, a move that could complicate prospects because that measure lacks sufficient support in the upper chamber.

data.title: Roche jumps despite reiterating 2026 outlook
data.description:
Roche (RHHBY) (RHHBF) shares closed ~5% higher in Zurich on Thursday, recording its best single-day gain since November, even as the Swiss drugmaker reaffirmed its 2026 outlook with its financials for the first half of the year.

The pharma giant recorded CHF 30.36B of revenue for H1 2026 with a 2% YoY decline, roughly in line with analysts’ expectations of CHF 30.31B, according to Reuters data. Its core operating profit also fell ~1% YoY to CHF 11.86B, but, on a forex-adjusted basis, rose ~10% YoY, topping Street forecasts.

The lackluster performance on a local currency basis was mainly due to appreciation of the Swiss franc against many foreign currencies, notably the USD, the company said, adding that demand for its pharmaceutical and diagnostic products drove topline growth.

Sales in Roche’s (RHHBF) Diagnostics unit fell ~3% YoY to CHF 6.7B, while its Pharmaceuticals segment added CHF 23.6B with a 1% YoY decline. As for notable products, eye therapy Vabysmo added CHF 2.0B, with ~8% YoY growth on a forex-adjusted basis but missing Street forecasts amid softer U.S. demand.

Multiple sclerosis drug Ocrevus brought in CHF 3.5B with a ~7% YoY growth, and Elevidys gene therapy, marketed with Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT), added CHF 180M with a ~62% YoY growth.

The company projected a CHF 600M headwind from off-patent versions of its brand-name pharmaceuticals this year compared to the $1B estimate previously but confirmed its growth outlook for 2026.

Roche (RHHBY) continues to estimate its adjusted EPS and sales to grow in a high-single-digit range and a mid-single-digit percentage range this year, respectively. “We’re trending toward the high end of our guidance,” CEO Thomas Schinecker told Bloomberg TV. “But let’s see how it continues” into Q3, he added.

data.title: Mortgage rates up for third straight week
data.description:
Mortgage rates rose for the third consecutive week, but more prospective homebuyers entered the market amid an improving inventory.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.58% as of July 23, up from 6.55% last week and down from 6.74% in the same period a year ago, according to the latest Freddie Mac (FMCC) Primary Mortgage Survey.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.96%, compared to 5.93% a week ago and 5.87% a year ago.

"As inventory improves in many markets, more prospective buyers are finding opportunities to enter the market even as borrowing costs remain elevated. While some economic uncertainty may persist in the coming months, housing demand and a growing supply of homes should continue to support purchase activity," said Mortgage Bankers Association's president and CEO Bob Broeksmit.

"As market conditions continue to evolve, borrowers should remember that shopping around for a mortgage rate can make a meaningful difference, potentially saving them thousands over the loan's lifetime," said Freddie Mac's chief economist, Sam Khater.

data.title: First Citizens projects $6B-$8B FDIC note paydown in Q3 2026 backed by BMO branch acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Citizens BancShares (FCNCA) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered strong second quarter performance, characterized by sequential top line growth that exceeded our guidance and consensus estimates." (Chairman & CEO Frank Holding)
  • "This morning, we reported adjusted net income of $691 million and adjusted earnings per share of $57.09, driving an adjusted ROE of 12.94% and an adjusted ROA of 1.18%." (Chairman & CEO Frank Holding)
  • "On the balance sheet, we delivered 1.6% sequential loan growth..." and "We delivered a 1.5% sequential increase in period-end deposits..." (Chairman & CEO Holding)
  • "Backed by a strong liquidity position, we prepaid another $2.5 billion of the FDIC purchase money note during the quarter, followed by an additional $1 billion in July." (Chairman & CEO Holding)
  • "In line with our guidance, net interest income increased by $35 million over the linked quarter..." (Chief Financial Officer Craig Nix)
  • "Adjusted noninterest income rose by $66 million sequentially, exceeding our guidance." (CFO Nix)

Outlook

  • "We are projecting third quarter loan balances in the range of $152 billion to $155 billion..." and "We reiterate our full year guidance of $153 billion to $157 billion, underpinned by sustained client activity and the upcoming BMO branch acquisition." (CFO Nix)
  • "We project third quarter deposits between $179 billion and $182 billion... adding approximately $5.3 billion in deposits." and "We reaffirm our full year guidance of $181 billion to $186 billion." (CFO Nix)
  • "Driven by recent wholesale funding activities and the anticipated positive liquidity event created by the BMO branch acquisition, we expect an acceleration in the pace of paydown in the third quarter totaling between $6 billion to $8 billion." (CFO Nix)
  • "On net interest income, we are maintaining our midpoint while marginally narrowing our full year range to $6.6 billion to $6.75 billion." and "We are guiding to a range of $1.63 billion to $1.71 billion in the third quarter." (CFO Nix)
  • "Reflecting our 2026 performance through the first half of the year, we are moving our full year net charge-off guidance to 30 to 35 basis points." (CFO Nix)

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted noninterest expense increased by $16 million sequentially..." and "The sequential increase was primarily driven by a $15 million targeted increase in marketing expense to maintain and attract new deposit balances in the direct bank." (CFO Nix)
  • "Period-end loans grew by $2.3 billion or 1.6% sequentially..." and "Global Fund Banking grew by $2.6 billion..." (CFO Nix)
  • "Period-end total deposits increased by $2.6 billion or 1.5% sequentially, driven by the direct bank, which added $2.8 billion during the quarter." (CFO Nix)
  • "The net charge-off ratio improved by 1 basis point sequentially to 29 basis points, outperforming our guidance." and "Nonaccrual loans held steady quarter-over-quarter at 96 basis points of total loans." (CFO Nix)
  • "As of July 21, we had repurchased over 20% of our common shares outstanding for a total of $6.3 billion, roughly 84% of our total authorization." and "our CET1 ratio was 10.77% at quarter end." (CFO Nix)

Q&A

  • Casey Haire, Autonomous Research: asked for updated NIM and deposit-cost context; CFO Nix: "For the third quarter... we’re expecting both baseline and ex accretion net interest income to be flat with the second quarter" and "our spot rate was 1.99%."
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous Research: asked about 2027 buybacks and Basel III; CFO Nix: "we expect that our CET1 ratio will be towards the higher end of our target range of 10% to 10.5% at the end of this year"; Unknown Executive: "we're certainly have more to share as we think about 2027 and pace as really -- that rule really kind of firms up."
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: asked about no-hike scenario and funding competition; CFO Nix: "If rates stay flat... we would still project flat net interest income" and "competition is very fierce for deposits"; Elliot Howard: "rates are kind of marginal cost in that channel, north of 4%."
  • Bernard Von Gizycki, Deutsche Bank: asked about FDIC note repayment sources and asset sensitivity; CFO Nix: "We have good capacity at the Federal Home Loan Bank" and "We are planning on doing some more long-term debt issuance"; Unknown Executive: "it is a fixed rate note... it will just provide us more flexibility."
  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: asked about sustainability of loan growth and loan pricing; Elliot Howard: "we would expect utilization to moderate, but... expect balances to grow" and "we started to see some moderation in some of that spread tightening."
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan: asked about SVB deposit trends and reserve release repeatability; President Marc Cadieux: "we continue to be pleased with the continued execution"; CFO Nix: "No, those are largely behind us, model enhancements."
  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: asked about using SVB deposits for FDIC paydown and Direct Bank pricing; Unknown Executive: "we are not bringing in any sort of that off-balance sheet product... to kind of support how we are looking at the forecast path"; CFO Nix: "The spot rate right now in the Direct Bank is 3.71%. The highest offered rate is 4.1%."
  • Christopher Marinac, Brean Capital: asked about more deposit acquisitions; CFO Nix: "We have no other current ones in the queue."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone centered on funding pressure, rate-path sensitivity, and capital return visibility, including "I'm worried about the fourth quarter" (Christopher McGratty, KBW) and repeated probing on buybacks and deposit pricing.
  • Slightly positive management tone emphasized stability and control points, including "we are pleased" (CFO Nix) and "we feel very confident in our ability to prepay the purchase money note" (CFO Nix), while acknowledging pressure: "competition is very fierce for deposits" (CFO Nix).
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management language shifted from caution around cuts (Q1: "The macroeconomic backdrop remains fluid") to more defined second-half expectations (Q2: "we expect an acceleration in the pace of paydown in the third quarter totaling between $6 billion to $8 billion").

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q2 profitability and credit commentary strengthened versus Q1, with Q2: "credit performance remains strong, exceeding our expectations" (Chairman & CEO Holding) versus Q1: "While lower rates were a headwind" (Chairman & CEO Holding).
  • Guidance framing changed from Q1’s rate-cut scenario ("zero to two 25 basis point rate cuts") to Q2’s potential hike path ("0 to 1, 25 basis point rate hike"). (CFO Nix)
  • Funding mix emphasis rotated: Q1 described leaning into broker deposits as Direct Bank marketing fell ("pivoted... to leverage lower cost broker deposits"), while Q2 highlighted Direct Bank deposit growth and marketing spend ("direct bank... added $2.8 billion" and "$15 million targeted increase in marketing expense"). (CFO Nix)
  • Q1 introduced a major strategic shift on branding ("transition to a united brand structure in the fourth quarter"), while Q2 messaging centered on execution, the BMO branch acquisition, and FDIC note paydown acceleration. (Chairman & CEO Holding; CFO Nix)

Risks and Concerns

  • "We observe that competition is very fierce for deposits" and "a lot of banks are putting out deposits that are really unprofitable." (CFO Nix)
  • "We are actively managing the commercial general office and innovation portfolios where we expect charge-offs to continue in the medium term." (CFO Nix)
  • "While we remain asset sensitive... the bulk of the net interest margin benefits will be realized in 2027 rather than late 2026" given deposit repricing speed. (CFO Nix)

Final Takeaway

Management portrayed Q2 as a quarter of stronger profitability, balance sheet growth led by Global Fund Banking, and resilient credit, while sharpening the second-half operating plan around the BMO branch acquisition, a faster FDIC purchase money note paydown in Q3, and a moderated buyback pace as capital approaches the 10% to 10.5% CET1 target range. Analysts focused on deposit competition, rate sensitivity, and the durability of NIM and capital return, with management reiterating stable near-term NII expectations and confidence in diversified funding levers to continue the FDIC note reduction.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Indra Sistemas, S.A. reports 1H results
data.description:
• Indra Sistemas, S.A. press release (ISMAF): 1H revenue of €3.18B (+29.8% Y/Y).
  • EBITDA surged 72.00% YoY to €456.00M with a 14.40% margin.
  • Net income reached €219.00M, up 2.10% year-over-year.
  • Record backlog reached €20.53B, more than doubling YoY.

data.title: Westamerica declares $0.48 dividend
data.description:
• Westamerica (WABC) declares $0.48/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 3.21%
  • Payable Aug. 14; for shareholders of record Aug. 3; ex-div Aug. 3.
  • See WABC Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Trump weighs larger military strikes on Iran - reports
data.description:
President Donald Trump is reportedly looking to resume major military operations in Iran, which could see strikes that are bigger than the ones during "Operation Epic Fury."

Trump told Axios he was close to deciding whether to authorize another military operation but had not reached a final decision. Two other U.S. officials said there have been no calls that have been made yet and no new orders have been given, Axios reported. 

"I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before. I am close to making a decision. We are all set for it," the president told Axios. 

Trump said Israel would also join the attack if he asked them, the report said. The president also added, "We don't need anybody" to launch a new operation against Tehran.

He also acknowledged that expanding military operations would have consequences, including the prospect of Iranian retaliation against Israel, according to the report.

The comments come as tensions in the region continue to rise following nearly two weeks of military exchanges between the United States and Iran. Oil markets have reacted sharply to the conflict, with Brent crude climbing above $100 a barrel amid concerns that further hostilities could disrupt energy supplies and shipping routes across the Middle East.

Axios also reported that Trump said Iran remains interested in negotiations but has yet to demonstrate enough willingness to reach an agreement. Two regional sources familiar with mediation efforts told the news outlet that Tehran had not accepted the latest proposal presented through intermediaries.

The statement comes after Trump warned Iran would face "major military punishment" if Yemen's Houthi militants strike Saudi vessels again.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
